---


Texas Instruments has a complicated ARM story. They bought Luminary Micro (Stellaris — the original Cortex-M3 family), rebranded as Tiva, then deprioritised it. MSP432 (Cortex-M4F low-power) was a play to extend the MSP430 legacy and was also discontinued. **Hercules** (Cortex-R safety) is alive and well in automotive and industrial.

<SpecSheet

/>

## Tiva C series (was Stellaris)

The first commercially shipping Cortex-M3 was Luminary Micro's. TI bought them, rebranded to Stellaris, then to Tiva C. The TM4C123 / TM4C129 lines are widely used in academic courses (the "TIVA Launchpad" was the educational board for years) and still in production for industrial designs.

- **TM4C123** — Cortex-M4F, 80 MHz, 256 KB flash, USB 2.0.
- **TM4C129** — Cortex-M4F, 120 MHz, 1 MB flash, Ethernet MAC+PHY on chip.

TI's marketing focus is now more on SimpleLink (BLE, WiFi) and SitaraLinux processors, but the Tiva parts remain available for legacy designs.

## MSP432 — discontinued

Marketed as the bridge from MSP430 (16-bit) to ARM Cortex-M4F. Was popular in academia. Discontinued for new designs in 2021. If you see it in older codebases, the migration target is typically Tiva C or a different vendor.

## Hercules — automotive Cortex-R

The **TMS570** and **RM48** Hercules lines are Cortex-R4F/R5F in **dual-core lockstep** with extensive BIST, ECC, and ASIL-D certification artefacts. Used in:

- Airbag electronic control units.
- Antilock braking systems (ABS).
- Electric power steering (EPS).
- Industrial robotics.

Hercules ships with HALCoGen (a graphical configurator that generates HAL code) and a full safety certification package (TÜV-SÜD-evaluated, ISO 26262 ASIL D ready). The toolchain is TI's CCS (Code Composer Studio, Eclipse-based with TI's compiler) or commercial Wind River / Green Hills.

For functional-safety work, Hercules + Wind River VxWorks is a textbook combination.

## SimpleLink — TI's wireless line

The **CC13xx** (sub-GHz + 2.4 GHz) and **CC26xx** (2.4 GHz / BLE) families are Cortex-M4 SoCs with integrated radio. Competitive with Nordic nRF52 for some BLE applications, with TI's strength being **sub-GHz** (915 MHz, 868 MHz) support that Nordic only added recently.

- **CC1352** — sub-GHz + 2.4 GHz, BLE + 802.15.4 + proprietary.
- **CC2640R2F / CC2650** — BLE-only.
- **CC2652R** — BLE + 802.15.4 (Thread / Zigbee).

The SimpleLink SDK is reasonably good — it's what you'll find in TI's reference designs and academic courses.

## When you'd pick TI ARM

- **Existing automotive program** with Hercules tooling — very strong functional-safety story.
- **Industrial with strict EMC requirements** — TI's analog and PCB recommendations are top-tier.
- **Sub-GHz wireless** — CC13xx still has the edge over Nordic in some sub-GHz applications.
- **You're heavily TI elsewhere** (analog ICs, power, MCUs) — supply chain consolidation is real value.

For greenfield consumer / IoT designs, ST or Nordic typically win on ecosystem.

## Hercules in depth — Cortex-R lockstep safety

Hercules is TI's flagship for IEC 61508 SIL-3 and ISO 26262 ASIL-D programs. The architectural pattern is **dual-core lockstep**: two identical Cortex-R4F or R5F cores execute the same instruction stream from a single fetch unit, with a small phase delay between them. A **CCM-R5** (CPU Compare Module) compares architectural state cycle-by-cycle and asserts an error if they diverge. The phase delay is critical — it ensures that a transient single-event upset (cosmic ray, EMI glitch) affects only one core's state at a time and is therefore caught by the comparator rather than corrupting both cores identically.

Beyond lockstep, Hercules ships with extensive built-in self-test:

- **LBIST** — Logic Built-In Self-Test, exercises every flip-flop in the CPU at boot and on demand.
- **PBIST** — Programmable Built-In Self-Test, tests every SRAM cell with march patterns.
- **ECC** — Single-Error-Correct / Double-Error-Detect on every memory and every bus.
- **Cross-bar parity** — every AHB / VBus path has parity protection.
- **Voltage and clock monitors** — independent oscillator that cross-checks the main PLL.
- **DCC** (Dual-Clock Comparator) — verifies the system clock against an independent reference; trips a fault if drift exceeds a threshold.

The certification artefacts shipped with each Hercules part — Safety Manual, FMEDA spreadsheets, DFA (Dependent Failure Analysis) reports — represent the bulk of the value. A functional-safety auditor reads the Safety Manual first; without it, even an excellent hardware design takes years to certify. SafeTI Diagnostic Library is the TI-provided runtime code that exercises these self-tests and reports fault status.

| Feature | TMS570LS | RM57L | RM48 |
|---------|----------|-------|------|
| Core | Cortex-R4F dual lockstep | Cortex-R5F dual lockstep | Cortex-R4F dual lockstep |
| Max clock | 220 MHz | 330 MHz | 220 MHz |
| Flash | up to 4 MB | up to 4 MB | up to 3 MB |
| Ethernet | yes (EMAC) | yes | optional |
| Target | EV inverters, BMS, ABS | EPS, ADAS pre-fusion | industrial automation, robotics |
| Cert level | SIL-3 / ASIL-D | SIL-3 / ASIL-D | SIL-3 / ASIL-D |

<Callout type="warn" title="Hercules toolchain choice has compliance implications">
The TI Arm Clang compiler ships with TÜV-SÜD qualification documents that map compiler version to verified output behaviour — required for ISO 26262 ASIL-D and IEC 61508 SIL-3 sign-off. Using `arm-none-eabi-gcc` instead is technically possible (the code builds fine) but you lose the qualification certificate and must produce your own, which is expensive. For certified programs, stay with CCS + TI Arm Clang. For prototypes, GCC is fine.
</Callout>

## SimpleLink wireless — the CC13xx / CC26xx architecture

SimpleLink wireless parts use a dual-core architecture that's important to understand. The **application core** is a Cortex-M4F (CC26x2, CC1352, CC2652) at up to 48 MHz, running the user application and most middleware. The **radio core** is a Cortex-M0 (or, on CC27xx, a Cortex-M33) running TI's RFCore firmware — a closed-source ROM image that implements PHY-layer packet handling. The two cores communicate through a doorbell interface and shared SRAM.

This architecture means BLE / 802.15.4 / Sub-GHz packet handling continues *even when the M4F is in deep sleep*. A typical BLE peripheral profile wakes the M4F only when a connection event requires application-layer processing; otherwise the M0 radio core handles connection maintenance, link-layer ACKs, and slave-latency timing. Current consumption while connected runs roughly 1–3 microamps average for a CC2640R2F.

CC1352 dual-band (sub-GHz + 2.4 GHz) is unique: the radio can switch bands within 100 microseconds, supporting concurrent multi-protocol designs where the same chip acts as a Thread border router and a 915 MHz mesh node. The newer **CC2674** adds Wi-SUN support — important for utility metering.

## Code Composer Studio and SimpleLink SDK

Code Composer Studio (CCS) is the TI-branded Eclipse IDE, with the TI Arm Clang compiler as the default toolchain. CCS supports SWO trace, ETM trace (on parts with the trace pins exposed), and TI's **XDS110** / **XDS200** debug probes that run TI's own protocol over USB. For most non-certified work, CCS is replaceable by VS Code + cortex-debug + arm-none-eabi-gcc with no real loss.

```bash
# SimpleLink SDK headless build
export SIMPLELINK_CC13X2_26X2_SDK=/path/to/sdk
make -C example/ble5_simple_peripheral
```

The SimpleLink SDK is decent — it uses TI's **TI-RTOS** (now SYS/BIOS, with a FreeRTOS option) as the kernel, with a board-specific HAL on top. The kernel selection is opinionated; if your team standardised on Zephyr, the migration cost is non-trivial.
